text: The surgeon begins by dissecting the omental adhesions attached to the colon and bladder, using robotic instruments to carefully separate these structures.

The surgeon sweeps the omentum to the upper abdomen and begins a medial to lateral dissection. The upper rectal dissection is performed to enhance bowel mobility for addressing the fistula. The surgeon plans to take the mesentery, preserving the left colic artery while taking the superior hemorrhoidal artery due to the benign nature of the disease.

The surgeon begins dissecting the fistula attachments and phlegmon from the left side wall, focusing on the area around the right round ligament and broad ligament. The dissection proceeds anteriorly to the fistula attachment on the bladder, with care taken to avoid enlarging the bladder hole. The surgeon encounters a residual abscess within the bladder wall, which aids in visualizing the dissection area. The fistula is visible as a protruding mucosal lip on the colon side, and the surgeon continues to carefully separate it while maintaining clear exposure and minimizing bleeding.

The surgeon navigates the deeper pelvis, identifying the uterus and attempting to separate the colon from its posterior surface. A significant amount of residual purulence is visible within the abscess cavity, and additional abscesses are noted. The IR drain is not yet visible, indicating incomplete drainage.
The surgeon locates the IR drain adjacent to the colon wall, highlighting the presence of a new paracolic abscess that developed spontaneously in the patient with a stable colovesicular fistula.

The surgeon encounters significant adherence to the left sidewall and attempts to safely dissect the area using a lateral approach. The uterus is visible, and the surgeon aims to visualize the left ureter while dissecting the residual chronic phlegmon on the iliacs and ureter. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to enhance visualization and ensure the ureter is not inadvertently damaged during the dissection.

The surgeon visualizes the deep pelvis, focusing on the thickened left mesorectum and the abscess and fistula areas involving the bladder and uterus. With the anatomical structures freed, the surgeon selects proximal and distal margins for resection, considering the condition of the bowel edges after mobilization. The splenic flexure is released using a lateral to medial approach.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field without changing the patient's position or redocking, using the existing arm positions to proceed. The dissection continues along the medial aspect of the left kidney up to the pancreas, addressing the high flexure adherent to the spleen. The surgeon carefully divides the proximal section with the aid of ICG to ensure a good margin.

The surgeon completes the distal bowel division using scissors and staples, ensuring two healthy edges with good blood supply for anastomosis. The specimen is moved aside, and a tension-free anastomosis is sewn beneath the chronic phlegmon.

The surgeon begins the anastomosis by sewing the posterior row, aligning the two staple lines of the bowel. A proctotomy and colotomy are made, followed by inner row suturing of the bowel lip adjacent to the staple lines. Suturing starts on the left side, ensuring precise placement of each suture, then proceeds to the right side, taking care not to obscure the view of the inner row.

The surgeon completes the inner anterior row of sutures by turning the suture around and cannelling over the top. The sutures are tied together in the midline to avoid joining at a corner, ensuring the edges of the bowel are imbricated to prevent exposed mucosa between the poles of the suture.

The surgeon cuts one suture and ties it to another, completing the anastomosis with a Lambert-style imbrication over the staple lines for added security. The original posterior suture is sewn to the silk stay suture, followed by a leak test to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis.

The surgeon pushes the anastomosis into the water and performs a leak test using a 19mm proctoscope, observing no bubbles and good distension, indicating a successful test. The surgical area is then cleaned.

The surgeon performs a bladder test by instilling methylene blue and saline into the bladder, observing for distension. Despite some residual blood from the fistula abscess cavity on the bladder dome, there is no leakage of methylene blue. A Blake drain is placed in the pelvis, and Surgicel is applied to the abscess cavity to control oozing. The pelvis is filled with omentum.
